categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: Belle Isle Marsh
description:
Join us to observe resident birds and fall migrants in a unique urban habitat that is a favorite for many bird species, including uncommon and rare birds. A Boston Nature Center naturalist will help us find and identify birds through field marks, sounds, and behaviors. Birders of all levels will enjoy these guided walks; beginning birders are encouraged to come! The trails are flat and easy to walk. This walk is co-sponsored with the Boston Nature center and the Brookline Bird Club. Meeting Place: RPM Fitness lot, 120 Banks Street; There is a chainlink fence with (worn out, not easy to see) signs authorizing public parking alongside the fence. Park in front of that fence. You’ll see a sign in the far back of the lot that says Belle Isle Marsh Marine Ecology Park. Meet in front of the sign. Please don’t park in a spot that says for gym only.

Please note preregistration is required and that participants must be at least 13 years of age and this outing is limited to 15 people.

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: Bear Creek Sanctuary, Saugus
description:
Bear Creek is an upland sanctuary built on the WINWaste (formerly Wheelabrator) landfill at the northeastern corner of Rumney Marsh in Saugus and Revere. Access information is below. Wear waterproof walking shoes and be prepared to hike 3 – 4 miles. The property is mostly very open, so be prepared for sun, wind and weather.

This walk is free and open to the public, but also note that this property is accessible by invitation only and visitors need to be chaperoned at all times. Please allow roughly 4 hours for this field trip. The actual length of the trip depends on conditions and the birds present. It’s usually difficult to break up the party if you need to be somewhere in the early afternoon. Come prepared to stay with the group

WINWaste company has graciously stocked up on some extra pairs of binoculars, so if you or someone you know wants to learn about birds and birding, come on down.

Please let Geoff Wilson s2ary@comcast.net know you’re coming so we can have a head count.

Bear Creek Sanctuary access is via the WINWaste plant, 100 Salem Turnpike (Rte 107), Saugus. The rotary at the intersection of Rtes 60 and 107 is about 1½ miles southwest of the plant and about 1 mile east of Rte 1 exit on Rte 60. When traveling northeast on 107, if you go over the Ballard Street bridge (just after a set of lights) you’ve gone too far — turn back. Here is a link to google maps:  http://tinyurl.com/326v87d.

When you arrive, drive through the plant gates, park in the parking lot immediately and wait for the group. Please do not drive farther into the property without an escort.

Ebird Hotspot List for Bear Creek Sanctuary

Trip Report Link

title: Woburn Horn Pond Conservation Area (Late Morning Walk)
description:
Join us for a later morning walk exploring the varied habitats of the Horn Pond Conservation Area. The walk will focus on the main pond, accompanying lagoons and marsh areas in search of migrating fall warblers and sparrows. Meeting Place: Sturgis Street entrance to Horn Pond. 9:00 – 10:30 AM

Registration not required.  For more info  Diana Fruguglietti

Horn Pond eBird List

title: Menotomy Bird Club celebrates 20 years of ‘focus on the moment’
description:
[et_pb_section fb_built=”1″ admin_label=”section” _builder_version=”4.16″ global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_row column_structure=”1_3,2_3″ admin_label=”row” _builder_version=”4.27.0″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_column type=”1_3″ _builder_version=”4.16″ custom_padding=”|||” global_colors_info=”{}” custom_padding__hover=”|||”][et_pb_image src=”https://bpx.ela.mybluehost.me/website_c5b9cabf/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Screenshot-2024-08-12-at-11.02.45 AM-1024×681.png” alt=”Nashville Warbler on Spruce branch” title_text=”ashville Warbler by Chris Coccone” _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”][/et_pb_image][et_pb_text _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”]Photo:  Nashville warbler by Christopher Ciccone[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type=”2_3″ _builder_version=”4.16″ custom_padding=”|||” global_colors_info=”{}” custom_padding__hover=”|||”][et_pb_text _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”]

Join us for our first fall meeting of 2024.

Experience the beauty of the natural world through the lens of local professional nature photographer (and MBC member) Christopher Ciccone, as he shares photos & stories of birds and other wildlife from around the globe… and around the corner.

Doors open at 6:30 for social time.  Meeting begins at 7:00 pm.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row column_structure=”2_5,3_5″ _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_column type=”2_5″ _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_text _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”][/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type=”3_5″ _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” global_colors_info=”{}”][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]

categories.description: club meetings and other events, both in person and on zoom

title: Horn Pond Sept 21st
description:
Join us we take a walk exploring the varied habitats of the Horn Pond Conservation Area. The walk will focus on the main pond, accompanying lagoons, marsh areas and Community Gardens in search of migrating fall warblers and sparrows.  Meeting Place: Sturgis Street entrance to Horn Pond. 7:30 – 9:30 AM Paul Ippolito Paul’s Email

Trip Report is here.

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: Foss Farm, Carlisle
description:
We’ll look for fall migrants such as sparrows and raptors in the community gardens and farm fields, including the old piggery, and also explore the wooded wetlands alongside the Concord River. Co-sponsored by the Carlisle Conservation Foundation. Meet at Foss Farm parking lot,just west of the bridge over the Concord River on route 225

Alan Ankers    alankers@comcast.net

Ebird hotspot Link

Trip Report (when available)

Co-sponsor

title: Millennium Park
description:
Join us to observe resident birds and early fall migrants in a unique urban habitat that is a favorite for many bird species, including uncommon and rare birds. A BNC naturalist will help us find and identify birds through field marks, sounds, and behaviors. Birders of all levels will enjoy these guided walks; beginning birders are encouraged to come! The trails are flat and easy to walk. Hats, waterproof footwear, insect repellent, and sunblock are recommended.

The walk is free but all participants must pre-register by emailing cgeorge@massaudubon.org and copying neil.calabro1@mac.com. Please note that participants must be at least 13 years of age and this outing is limited to 30 people.

Directions:  300 Gardner St, Boston. Meet in the parking lot nearest the canoe launch. After entering the park, stay left (don’t drive up the big hill). Immediately take another left. You may see signs for Canoe Launch. Follow that road to the end of the canoe launch parking lot.

eBird Link https://ebird.org/hotspot/L350679

Cosponsor:

title: Wright-Locke Farm, Winchester Sept 24
description:
Join us as we explore parts of this 20 acre community farm that includes historic farm buildings, growing fields; pasture lands to the north of the farmstead and wooded areas to the west abutting the Whipple Hill and Locke Conservation Areas. Let’s see what gems are hidden away in this new area.

Meeting Place: Park and meet at Mullen field located at 54 Ridge St. Winchester. This lot is across the street from the Farm; the Farm is located at 82 Ridge St, Winchester.

Ebird Hotspot Link

Trip Report with photos is available.

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: Sandy Beach, Winchester
description:
Sandy Beach (Charles Shannon Beach), a small DCR park at the northern end of the Mystic Lakes in Winchester, attracts a large variety of birds. In the fall, migrating warblers can be found in the woods, while wintering waterfowl start to arrive on the lakes. Meet in the large parking lot on Mystic Valley Parkway. 7:30-9:00 AM Renee LaFontaine dacooper@tiac.net

(photo by Toby Sackton at Sandy Beach June 2021)

eBird hotspot link: Sandy Beach

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: Arlington Resevoir
description:
The Arlington Reservoir is a stopping point for a number of migrating ducks and shorebirds. Join us for a walk around the Reservoir where we should find a good variety of waterfowl and other birds. We’ll make a circuit around the reservoir and check out Lexington Community Farm and its field edges to see what we can find.

Co-sponsored with the Brookline Bird Club. Meet at the Lowell Street parking lot. 7:30 – 9:30AM

John Edmondson Johnhedmondson@gmail.com

Co-sponsor

 

 

eBird hotspot: https://ebird.org/hotspot/L358557

Trip Report is here

title: Dunback Meadow Oct 6th
description:
Join us we explore Dunback Meadow in Lexington searching for migrating fall warblers and sparrows with possibilities such as Lincoln Sparrow, Clay-colored Sparrow, Indigo Bunting and we can always hope for an elusive Dickcissel or Fox Sparrow.

Meeting Place:  Allen Street entrance to Dunback Meadows in Lexington. There is parking along Allen Street. 8:00 – 10:00 AM Paul Ippolito paulippolito295@gmail.com

eBird Hotspot Dunback Meadow

title: Carlisle Cranberry Bog Oct 19th
description:
We’ll search the area Carlisle’s Cranberry Bog for newly-arrived winter visitors and late fall migrants — waterbirds, raptors and songbirds.

Co-sponsored by the Carlisle Conservation Foundation.

Meet at the Cranberry Bog House, 750 Curve Street. 8:00-10:30AM Alan Ankers alankers@comcast.net

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

title: MBC Meeting: Feature length Documentary ‘The Piping Plovers of Moonlight Bay’ Oct 22nd
description:
[et_pb_section fb_built=”1″ theme_builder_area=”post_content” _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default”][et_pb_row _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” theme_builder_area=”post_content”][et_pb_column _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” type=”4_4″ theme_builder_area=”post_content”][et_pb_text _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” theme_builder_area=”post_content” hover_enabled=”0″ sticky_enabled=”0″]

The Menotomy Bird Club will be hosting a meeting on Tuesday, October 22nd @ 6:30 PM at the Jenks Center, 109 Skillings Road in Winchester as we will have Kim Smith; a documentary filmmaker, wildlife conservationist, lecturer, photojournalist, author, illustrator, and an award-winning landscape designer will present, “The Piping Plovers of Moonlight Bay”,  a feature-length documentary premiering at film festivals in September 2024. At the time of this writing, the documentary has been accepted at seven film festivals and was awarded Best Family Friendly Film at the Cine Paris Film Festival.

Doors open at 6:00 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 6:30 PM.

From the Director Kim Smith:

The Piping Plovers of Moonlight Bay is a narrated documentary that tells the  story of Pippi and Big Papi Plover and their blended family of chicks. Told through the adventures of an extraordinary pair of birds, experience the life story of the Piping Plover, from courtship to nesting to fledging to migration. Massachusetts is at the forefront of Piping Plover recovery and the film is fittingly set against the stage of the north of Boston coastline.  Learn how we can all become stewards of these vulnerable, yet courageous, tiny threatened nesting shorebirds.

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_code _builder_version=”4.27.0″ _module_preset=”default” theme_builder_area=”post_content” hover_enabled=”0″ sticky_enabled=”0″][/et_pb_code][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]

title: Fresh Pond, Cambridge Nov 9th
description:
An easy 2-mile walk through pondside meadows and forest paths at Fresh Pond. The over-wintering waterbirds have started to arrive, and we may see Ring-necked Ducks, Ruddy Ducks and both Hooded and Common Mergansers. Meadow and forest species may include year-round residents as well as seasonal visitors from the north (e.g. White-throated Sparrows, Juncos).

Note on Parking:

We will meet in the parking lot of the TD Bank at Fresh Pond Circle, located at the intersection of Concord Avenue with Fresh Pond Parkway in Cambridge. If you are driving, please see the walk leader (Thomas Michel) when you arrive to get a parking permit to place on the dashboard of your car, and then park in the Trader Joe’s lot. If you don’t have a permit, your car may be towed.

categories.name: MBC Trips
categories.description: trips led or sponsored by MBC

categories.name: Other Local Birding Trips
categories.description: other local birding trips of interest

title: Millennium Park Nov 10th
description:
Join us to observe resident birds and early fall migrants in a unique urban habitat that is a favorite for many bird species, including uncommon and rare birds. A BNC naturalist will help us find and identify birds through field marks, sounds, and behaviors. Birders of all levels will enjoy these guided walks; beginning birders are encouraged to come! The trails are flat and easy to walk. Hats, waterproof footwear, insect repellent, and sunblock are recommended.

The walk is free but all participants must pre-register by emailing cgeorge@massaudubon.org and copying neil.calabro1@mac.com. Please note that participants must be at least 13 years of age and this outing is limited to 30 people.

Directions:  300 Gardner St, Boston. Meet in the parking lot nearest the canoe launch. After entering the park, stay left (don’t drive up the big hill). Immediately take another left. You may see signs for Canoe Launch. Follow that road to the end of the canoe launch parking lot.

eBird Link https://ebird.org/hotspot/L350679

Cosponsor:

title: Bear Creek Sanctuary, Saugus Nov 17th
description:
Bear Creek is an upland sanctuary built on the WINWaste (formerly Wheelabrator) landfill at the northeastern corner of Rumney Marsh in Saugus and Revere. Access information is below. Wear waterproof walking shoes and be prepared to hike 3 – 4 miles. The property is mostly very open, so be prepared for sun, wind and weather.

This walk is free and open to the public, but also note that this property is accessible by invitation only and visitors need to be chaperoned at all times. Please allow roughly 4 hours for this field trip. The actual length of the trip depends on conditions and the birds present. It’s usually difficult to break up the party if you need to be somewhere in the early afternoon. Come prepared to stay with the group

WINWaste company has graciously stocked up on some extra pairs of binoculars, so if you or someone you know wants to learn about birds and birding, come on down.

Please let Geoff Wilson s2ary@comcast.net know you’re coming so we can have a head count.

Bear Creek Sanctuary access is via the WINWaste plant, 100 Salem Turnpike (Rte 107), Saugus. The rotary at the intersection of Rtes 60 and 107 is about 1½ miles southwest of the plant and about 1 mile east of Rte 1 exit on Rte 60. When traveling northeast on 107, if you go over the Ballard Street bridge (just after a set of lights) you’ve gone too far — turn back. Here is a link to google maps:  http://tinyurl.com/326v87d.

When you arrive, drive through the plant gates, park in the parking lot immediately and wait for the group. Please do not drive farther into the property without an escort.

Ebird Hotspot List for Bear Creek Sanctuary

Trip Report  here

title: MBC Meeting:  Across Namibia – Birds and Other Cool Stuff
description:
The Menotomy Bird Club will be hosting our next  meeting on Tuesday, November 19th @ 7:00 PM at the Jenks Center. We will have Alan Ankers presenting his photographic tour covering the entire breadth of Namibia in southwestern Africa exploring the country’s history, geography, animals, plants, reptiles and insects, as well as birds. Plenty of birds!

Alan Ankers has keenly birded Middlesex County for the last thirty years and in recent years started exploring the rest of the world’s birds. Since co-leading the very first Menotomy Bird Club outing in March 2003, he has led regular bird walks for the club in his current hometown of Carlisle. He enjoys hiking and generally exploring the natural world, particularly dragonflies and butterflies as well as birds.

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.

title: MBC Meeting:  Climbing with Hawks and Owls and protecting Raptor Nests
description:
We have our next meeting, Tuesday, December 17th @ 7:00 PM at the Jenks Center, 109 Skillings Road in Winchester as we will have Andrew Joslin, a professional tree climber, naturalist, and artist talking about his climbing experiences with our hawks and owls and the difficult problem of rodenticide threats to our local raptors.  Andrew has become the go-to in eastern Massachusetts for getting stuck cats out of trees. This expertise led to wildlife rehabilitators reaching out to Andrew for assistance with raptor nest problems.

 

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.

Photo: Cooper’s Hawk seen on MBC walk on Brooks Estate April 10, 2024 by Toby Sackton

title: MBC Meeting:  The Amazing Migration of Birds
description:
Dave Williams will be talking about the migration of birds, one of the most amazing aspects of natural history. As we will explore how birds migrate to and from their breeding grounds, we will see how technology has shed new light on these awe-inspiring journeys. Some birds migrate more than 20,00 miles! Other birds may only travel several hundred miles. The how’s and whys of migration will be discussed using animated maps, slides, video, and more.

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.

Photo: Cooper’s Hawk seen on MBC walk on Brooks Estate April 10, 2024 by Toby Sackton

title: Join our Trip to Manu and Machu Pichu Southern Peru Nov 2025
description:
Join MBC leader Diana Frugulietti on a November birding trip to Manu, Peru and Machu Pichu. the trip has eight spaces, and will run if enough people sign up.  Diana says “the minimum to run would be 3 people. But the more, the less money we pay per person. Please let me know if you are interested and if there are any days in November you can’t go. As you will read, booking needs to happen in the next month or so since Machu Pichu books up in advance.”

NOTE THIS TRIP WILL RUN IN NOVEMBER, BUT YOU MUST EXPRESS INTEREST AND SIGN UP BY MARCH TO SECURE SPACE.  THAT IS WHY THE DATE LISTING IS IN MARCH.

Details:

Day 01: WELCOME TO CUSCO

Upon arrival meet your guide and travel to the Wetlands of Huacarpay for Andean birds such as Puna Teal, Puna Ibis, Yellow-winged Black-bird and others. Continue to San Salvador community along the Urubamba Valley where we may have excellent looks of the endemic Bearded Mountaineer, others include Giant Hummingbird, Sparkling Violet-ear and more. Visit to the impressive site of Pisac. Afternoon arrival to hotel in the heart of the Urubamba Valley. Night in the Urubamba Valley. (Box-lunch)

Day 02: MACHUPICCHU

Early morning transfer to Ollantaytamo Train station to catch morning train (1 hours and 30 mins ride) upon arrival catch bus to the site of Machupicchu and explore it in the company of your guide. Lunch. Afternoon train back to Ollantaytambo, upon arrival transfer back to hotel. Night in the Urubamba Valley. (B, L)

Day 03: MALAGA PASS TO CUSCO

Early morning visit of hummer garden where we may see up to 12 species including the fabulous Sword-billed, Giant, Tyrian and others. Continue to the Cusco/Quillabamba road with it’s highest point the Malaga Pass (14,200 ft) the scenery is quite dramatic and gives us chances to explore various habitats where we may encounter the endemics Creamy-crested Spinetail, White-tufted Sunbeam, Unstreaked Tit-tyrant, Cuzco Brushfinch others include Cream-winged Cinclodes, Andean Goose and more. Late afternoon arrival to Cusco. Night in Cusco. (B, Box-Lunch)

Day 04: CUSCO TO THE CLOUD FOREST

Early departure to the famous Manu Road, today we will make our way to the cloud forest making selected stops on the way looking for Andean Guan, Andean Motmot, colorful mountain tanagers hummingbirds and more. Afternoon arrival to lodge. Night at Cock of the Rock Lodge (confirmation will depend on availability). (B, Box-Lunch)

Day 05 and Day 06: AT THE CLOUD FOREST

Full days spend exploring the lower and upper section of the Andes with a big variety of habitats in search of Blue-winged Mountain Tanager, Golden-olive Woodpecker, beauties such as Long-tailed Sylph, Violet-fronted Brilliant, the handsome Wire-crested Thorntail and many more. We will also have the opportunity to visit probably the most accessible Andean Cock of the Rock lek to witness their morning mating rituals. Night at the clorest. (B, L, D)

Day 07: THE CLOUD FOREST TO THE FOOTHILLS

Morning departure to explore the lower part of the Andes, here we will be changing in habitats dramatically allowing to see other species that don’t occur  higher such as various species of parakeets, macaws, trogons, antbirds, jacamars and more. Night spend at Manu Biostation. (Confirmation will depend on availability). (B, L, D)

Day 08: THE FOOTHILLS

Day spend exploring forest trails as well as visiting hummingbird gardens and bird feeding stations, we have good chances to see the very shy Undulated Tinamu, the adorable Rufous-crested Coquette, Lemon-throated Barbet and more. Night at lodge. (B, L, D)

Day 09: THE FOOTHILLS TO UPPER MONTANE FOREST

Morning birding around lodge grounds before breakfast for whatever we may have missed days before. Continue our drive along the famous Manu Road to our next inn the Wayquecha Biostation (confirmation will depend on availability). Quality stops will be made on the way for mixed-flocks and perched birds, we hope to come across Blue-banded Toucanet, Masked Trogon, Hooded Mountain Tanager and more. Afternoon arrival to lodge. (B, Box-Lunch, D)

Day 10: UPPPER MONTANE FOREST

Full day exploration of this fabulous habitat which offers good opportunities to spot rather shy birds such as Golden-collared, Scarlet-bellied, Grass-Green Tanagers, Gray-breasted Mountain-Toucan and many more. Time will also be spent exploring one of lodge trails where we have good chances to spot the endemic Urubamba Antpitta. Night at lodge. (B, L, D)

Day 11: UPPER MONTANE FOREST BACK TO CUSCO

Morning birding before breakfast and start our drive back to Cusco on time to catch afternoon flights back to Lima and then home. (B, Box-Lunch)

END OF SERVICES

-03 paying travelers USD 4,750

-04 to 06 paying travelers USD 4,150

-07 to 08 paying travelers USD 3,500

-Single Supplement USD 400

 

The above prices includes as follows:

-10 nights lodging at 3* stars hotels while in the Urubamba Valley and Cusco, best available lodges along the Manu Road (Cock of the Rock Lodge, Manu Biostation and Wayquecha Biostation) all the confirmations depend on availability at standard rooms

-Private transport according to program while in the Urubamba Valley and the Manu Road

-Professional birding guide for tour length

-Local history/archaeology guide to visit Machupicchu on day 02 of itinerary for around 03 hours

-All breakfasts served at hotels

-Full board once at lodges (B, L, D)

-03 box-lunches

-One menu lunch on day 02 of itinerary (Machupicchu)

-Entrance fees to visit sites detailed in itinerary (Pisac)

-One time entrance fee to various hummingbird gardens and bird feeding stations while in the Urubamba Valley and the Manu Road

-One time visit to Andean Cock of the Rock Lodge along the Manu Road

-Round-trip Train Tickets to Machupicchu on (Vistadome, Explorer, Voyager service) confirmation depends on availability

-One time entrance fee to Machupicchu (morning shift)

-Round-trip bus tickets to reach the archaeological site of Machupicchu from  Aguas Calientes

-All lodging (Urubamba Valley, Cusco and Manu Road), transportation (General tour, Train Tickets to Machupicchu, buses), entrances fees (Pisac and Machupicchu) and box-lunches

-Lodging and meals for guide and driver along the Manu Road

-Water and snacks while on tour except for Machupicchu

-Round-trip transfer from hotel in the Urubamba Valley to Ollantaytambo Train Station to catch train to Machupicchu

 

Does not include:

-Not mentioned visits

-Additional visit to garden and bird feeding stations

-Dinners and not mentioned lunches

-Domestic airfare between Lima/Cusco/Lima

-Laundry

-Tips and other gratuities

-Alcoholic drinks

-Fees for money wires

-Payments with credit cards with up to 4.66% commission depending on type or card

title: MBC Meeting:  Meet the Bird Observer Magazine with Editor Marsha Salett
description:
For more than 50 years, Bird Observer has been the local New England journal for birders of all skill levels. Their mission is to support and promote the observation, understanding, and conservation of the wild birds of New England through bimonthly print and online journals. Marsha C. Salett, editor of Bird Observer and a teacher/naturalist at Mass Audubon’s Broadmoor Wildlife Sanctuary in South Natick for 30 years, will present an overview of the variety of original articles they publish: conservation and natural history, where to go birding, regional rarities, book reviews, field notes, bird sightings, photographs, artwork, and more.

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.

Photo: Cover of the Bird Observer Magazine from August, 2024

title: MBC Meeting:  A practical guide to restoring pollinator-generated biodiversity
description:
Please join the Menotomy Bird Club on Tuesday, April 29th @ 7:00 PM at the Jenks Center, 109 Skillings Road in Winchester as we will have Robert J. Gegear an Associate Professor in the Department of Biology at UMass Dartmouth presenting – More than just the buzz:  A practical guide to restoring pollinator-generated biodiversity (and why it matters)

 

Plant-pollinator diversity is declining at an alarming rate across New England, pushing many species to the brink of local extinction unless urgent conservation measures are implemented. In this talk, Robert will discuss using species-level ecological data to vastly improve recommended plant lists aimed at restoring bumblebee and butterfly diversity on degraded landscapes including how ‘Beecologist’ citizen scientists have used data-driven conservation approach to successfully restore pollination diversity ranging from home gardens to multi-acre conservation lands.

 

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.

title: Horn Pond Saturday May 10
description: Join us as we explore the varied habitat of the Horn Pond Conservation area. As we are in a peak period for migration, on this unique walk, we will be searching the entire area of Horn Pond in search of as many species as we can find in this ½ day of birding.  We will be searching for migrating warblers and resident birds such as Orchard Orioles, Swallows, Eastern Phoebes, Warbling Vireos, Yellow Warblers and Rose-breasted Grosbeaks. As we are trying to find as many species as possible, we will be moving at a much faster pace than normal and traveling up Horn Pond Mountain. Thus please plan accordingly as the walk will have uneven terrain; muddy areas are expected due to the recent rains. Meet at the Sturgis Street entrance 7:00 AM – 11:30 AM Paul Ippolito

title: MBC Meeting:  Finches of the US and Canada with Lillian Stokes
description:
From goldfinches to crossbills, from backyards to wilderness peaks, finches are one of the most exciting, mysterious, and beloved group of songbirds. In this lively, information-packed talk featuring stunning photography, Lillian Stokes, best-selling author of 35 Stokes guides, will introduce you to the 43 finches of the United States and Canada from feeder-favorite goldfinches to Red Crossbill tribes, to least known mountain-top Black Rosy Finches to endangered Hawaiian Finches with their own “Darwin’s finches”-like story.

The talk will cover finch identification, behavior, irruptions, how to attract and conserve finches and more. Sprinkled throughout with quotes from the creative Quick Take chapter intros, filled with amazing facts, scientific knowledge and gorgeous photos, this talk will leave you understanding so much more about the finches you thought you knew.

Abut Lillian Stokes:

Lillian Stokes is an award-winning author and photographer. Here latest book, hailed as one of the Best Bird Books of 2024 by ABA, is The Stokes Guide to Finches of the United States and Canada with coauthor Matthew Young. Lillian and her husband Don produced 35 Stokes Guides selling over 5 million copies including the bestselling Stokes Field Guide to the Birds of North America, the Stokes Field Guide to Birds: Eastern and Western Regions, the Stokes Beginner’s Guide to Birds, the Stokes Nature Guides, and the Stokes Backyard Nature Books. Lillian was producer and cohost of two national PBS birdwatching TV series seen by 40 million viewers. Other achievements and activities include; Federal Duck Stamp Judge, Partners in Flight National Conservation Award, and Lifetime Achievement Award Kappa Kappa Gamma, Board of Directors of the Hawk Migration Association of North America and Nuttall Ornithological Club, former Board member NH Audubon.

Doors open at 6:30 for set up and social time and the meeting starts promptly at 7:00 PM.
